Lab Guide › Working in the Lab

Work expectations

Work hours, flexibility, compensation, and how we manage projects and deadlines.

Getting Started

  • Start guide: Undergraduates
  • Start guide: Graduate students
  • Start guide: Postdocs

  • Time off & vacation

Professional Development

  • Attending a conference
  • Applying for funding
  • Request recommendation letters
  • File your dissertation

The lab is an equal-opportunity employer. All opportunities are posted publicly so any qualified person can apply.

Work hours

  • Graduate students are asked to maintain at least a 40-hour work week, including coursework and research. Early on, more time goes to classes; that balance shifts over time.
  • Undergraduate students are asked to commit about 10 hours per week. This can be sporadic, as these are often volunteer positions. Keep your grades up, and let Christina know if you need to adjust in a given week.
  • Flexible hours are supported for everyone. There are no fixed expectations about when or how long you are in the office, as long as the work gets done and your project is making good progress.
  • Lab members are not expected to work weekends.
  • If you are unwell — emotionally or physically — please take care of yourself. Illness is not a personal failing.
  • See Time off & vacation for leave policies.

If you’re unsure whether you’re working enough, too much, or on the right things, try a time audit for 2–3 weeks. Tools like Toggl Track can help.

Compensation

  • PhD students are paid. MSc students are not guaranteed payment, but it is prioritized in the budget.
  • If funds are available, undergraduates may be paid after a one-quarter trial period, provided they can dedicate the time and meet expectations set with their supervisor.

Project management

  • Each student is expected to manage their own project — setting and meeting targets — with support to build those skills.
  • We use Gantt charts, Kanban boards, and similar tools to take an intentional approach to research project management.

Deadlines

  • Set and respect internal deadlines to avoid last-minute crunches.
  • Give as much advance notice as possible. Christina aims to finish tasks at least a week before deadlines; with enough lead time, she will do her best to avoid working right up to the wire.

If there are substantial differences between our working styles, we’ll discuss them directly in a one-on-one to set an intentional plan for working together effectively.
